name: System.Actor
comment:
# Actor

In the context of this package an Actor is a Component that is mapped to the System.

Currently there are three components defined;

  - The Worker Component
  - The Ui Component
  - The Layout Component

An Actor probably looks very familiar to you,
that is because it closely resembles the Elm Program definition.

It should be relativaly straight forwards to map you exisiting Elm component on top
of one of the existing Components.

Actors can communicate directly with other Actors only when it knows it's PID.

@docs Actor

@docs SystemActor, toSystemActor

aliases.name: Actor
aliases.comment: An Actor looks a lot like a Browser.element !
aliases.type: { init : ( System.Internal.PID.PID, Json.Decode.Value ) -> ( actorModel, msg ), update : model -> msg -> System.Internal.PID.PID -> ( actorModel, msg ), view : model -> System.Internal.PID.PID -> (System.Internal.PID.PID -> Maybe.Maybe output) -> output, subscriptions : model -> System.Internal.PID.PID -> Platform.Sub.Sub msg, events : System.Internal.Event.Event -> System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Event.EventHandler msg }

aliases.name: SystemActor
aliases.comment:
An Actor within the System has a different Type.

You can create SystemActors using the `toSystemActor` function.

values.name: toSystemActor
values.comment: Apply your model over your Actor to create a SystemActor
values.type: System.Actor.Actor model actorModel output msg -> model -> System.Actor.SystemActor actorModel output msg

name: System.Browser
comment: This module helps you set up an System Program. # Elements Create an HTML element managed by Elm. @docs element # Applications Create an application that manages Url changes. When the application starts, init gets the initial Url. You can show different things depending on the Url! When someone clicks a link, like Home, it always goes through onUrlRequest. The resulting message goes to your update function, giving you a chance to save scroll position or persist data before changing the URL yourself with pushUrl or load. More info on this in the UrlRequest docs! When the URL changes, the new Url goes through onUrlChange. The resulting message goes to update where you can decide what to show next. Applications always use the Browser.Navigation module for precise control over Url changes. @docs application

values.name: application
values.comment: Create an Application managed by Elm through a System
values.type: { apply : actorModel -> System.Internal.SystemActor.SystemActor actorModel output (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg), factory : actorName -> ( System.Internal.PID.PID, Json.Decode.Value ) -> ( actorModel, System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg ), init : flags -> Url.Url -> Browser.Navigation.Key -> List.List (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg), view : List.List output -> List.List (Html.Html (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg)), onUrlRequest : Browser.UrlRequest -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg, onUrlChange : Url.Url -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg, onLogMessage : System.Internal.Message.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg } -> System.Platform.Program flags address actorName actorModel wrappedMsg

values.name: element
values.comment: Create an HTML element managed by Elm through a System.
values.type: { apply : actorModel -> System.Internal.SystemActor.SystemActor actorModel output (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg), factory : actorName -> ( System.Internal.PID.PID, Json.Decode.Value ) -> ( actorModel, System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg ), init : flags -> List.List (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg), view : List.List output -> Html.Html (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg), onLogMessage : System.Internal.Message.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg } -> System.Platform.Program flags address actorName actorModel wrappedMsg

name: System.Component.Worker
comment:
# Worker

A Worker is a [headless](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Headless_software) Actor, it has no user interface.

This is great if you want to use an Actor as the “brain” for something else.

@docs Worker, toActor

aliases.name: Worker
aliases.comment: The Type of a Worker Component
aliases.type: { init : ( System.Process.PID, Json.Decode.Value ) -> ( model, List.List msgOut, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msgIn ), update : msgIn -> model -> ( model, List.List msgOut, Platform.Cmd.Cmd msgIn ), subscriptions : model -> Platform.Sub.Sub msgIn, events : System.Event.ComponentEventHandlers msgIn }

values.name: toActor
values.comment: Create an Actor from a Worker
values.type: System.Component.Worker.Worker model msgIn msgOut -> { wrapModel : model -> actorModel, wrapMsg : msgIn -> wrappedMsg, mapIn : wrappedMsg -> Maybe.Maybe msgIn, mapOut : System.Process.PID -> msgOut -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg } -> System.Actor.Actor model actorModel (Html.Html msg) (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg)

name: System.Log

The opaque LogMessage type

You can create LogMessages using the helper function available

values.comment: LogMessage to Description
values.type: System.Log.LogMessage address actorName appMsg -> { posix : Maybe.Maybe Time.Posix, severity : System.Log.Severity, pid : System.Internal.PID.PID, message : Maybe.Maybe (System.Message.SystemMessage address actorName appMsg), description : String.String }

values.name: toString
values.comment:
Turn a LogMessage into a String

    error pid wrappedMsg "I'm sorry Dave, I'm afraid I can't do that"
        |> toString

    -- error | 2(1) | I'm sorry Dave, I'm afraid I can't do that

    error system wrappedMsg "I'm sorry Dave, I'm afraid I can't do that"
        |> withPosix now
        |> toString

    -- 2019/12/25 12:59:59 (UTC) | error | system | I'm sorry Dave, I'm afraid I can't do that
values.type: System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> String.String

values.name: warning
values.comment: Create a new LogMessage with the severity Warning
values.type: System.Internal.PID.PID -> String.String -> System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg

values.name: withMessage
values.comment:
Supply a Message that resulted in this logMessage

You could use this to retry a failed command for instance.
values.type: System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> System.Message.SystemMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg

values.name: withPosix
values.comment: Add a Posix (elm/time) to your LogMessage
values.type: System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg -> Time.Posix -> System.Log.LogMessage address actorName wrappedMsg

name: System.Message

aliases.name: SystemMessage
aliases.comment: The type of the System Messages

values.name: batch
values.comment: Batch perform a list of messages
values.type: List.List (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg) -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: ignoreLog
values.comment: convenience function that ignores all logs
values.type: System.Internal.Message.LogMessage address actorName appMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: kill
values.comment:
Kill a process

This will trigger the Actors onKill event on which you can decide what to do with this Message.

There is a Default behaviour available that will remove the Process from the System.
values.type: System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: log
values.comment:
Log a LogMessage

This will trigger the onLogMessage function you provided while initializing your application.
values.type: System.Internal.Message.LogMessage address actorName appMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: noOperation
values.comment:
Don't do anythinge

This can be handy for instance when you want to spawn an Actor but don't do anything with it's resulting PID.

    spaw MyActorWorker (always noOperation)
values.type: System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: populateAddress
values.comment:
Add a PID to the System's view

The System will render views in the order it receives it.
values.type: address -> System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: populateView
values.comment:
Add a PID to the System's view

The System will render views in the order it receives it.
values.type: System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: sendToAddress
values.comment: Send a message to an Address
values.type: address -> appMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: sendToPid
values.comment: Send a message to a PID.
values.type: System.Internal.PID.PID -> appMsg -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: spawn
values.comment: Spawn an Actor
values.type: actorName -> (System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg) -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: spawnWithFlags
values.comment: Spawn an Actor with given flags (as an encoded JSON Value)
values.type: Json.Encode.Value -> actorName -> (System.Internal.PID.PID -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg) -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

values.name: toCmd
values.comment: Converts a msg in to Cmd.
values.type: msg -> Platform.Cmd.Cmd msg

values.name: updateDocumentTitle
values.comment: Update the document title
values.type: String.String -> System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName appMsg

aliases.name: Program
aliases.comment:
This will be the type of your program when you create it using this package.

Checkout out the `element` and `application` functions in the System.Browser module to find out how to create a System.Program.

_A [Program](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/elm/core/latest/Platform#Program) describes an Elm program! How does it react to input? Does it show anything on screen? Etc._
aliases.type: Platform.Program flags (System.Internal.Model.Model address actorName actorModel) (System.Internal.Message.Message address actorName wrappedMsg)

aliases.name: PID
aliases.comment: Each Process has an unique identifier ([PID](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Process_identifier)).

values.name: equals
values.comment: Check if two PID's are the same
values.type: System.Process.PID -> System.Process.PID -> Basics.Bool
